fingerprint: validation:the contract claims it covers 'exactly three production affordance sites' that hardcode --agent codex, but no acceptance criterion or validation command proves that claim repo-wide. the rg guard is scoped only to the five named files (resolve.go, errors.go, prompt.go, prompt_test.go, affordances_test.go). a fourth lifecycle affordance emitting 'pactum execute plan ... --agent codex' in any other file/package would let the contract pass fully green while the goal (no user is steered to codex) stays partially unmet. add a repo-wide grep excluding the legitimately-retained skill.go/skill_test.go install-target text to turn the 'exactly three' assertion into a checked invariant.
message: The contract claims it covers 'exactly three production affordance sites' that hardcode --agent codex, but no acceptance criterion or validation command proves that claim repo-wide. The rg guard is scoped only to the five named files (resolve.go, errors.go, prompt.go, prompt_test.go, affordances_test.go). A fourth lifecycle affordance emitting 'pactum execute plan ... --agent codex' in any other file/package would let the contract pass fully green while the goal (no user is steered to codex) stays partially unmet. Add a repo-wide grep excluding the legitimately-retained skill.go/skill_test.go install-target text to turn the 'exactly three' assertion into a checked invariant.

fingerprint: validation:the contract's enumerated test changes contradict what its validation commands require. the narrative says affordances_test.go has 'all three' sites to update (wantnext builder, the assertnext line, human next: substring), but the changed affordance (resolve.go:282 prompt_built / errors.go:87 / prompt.go:563) is the common source feeding six assertion sites: affordances_test.go lines 112, 231, 237, 261, 277, and 355 (status.next at 261 and show.next at 277 expose the same prompt_built next array; there are two assertnext lines, 231 and 355). both `go test ./internal/...` and the rg guard require all six to change. an implementer who follows the 'all three' count literally will produce a failing gate (go test fails at 261/277/355; rg guard still matches them). the validation suite is sound and catches the gap, so this is advisory rather than blocking, but the contract's stated test scope is not self-consistent with its own validation and the undercount invites a wasted gate-fix round-trip. fix: correct the enumeration to all six occurrences (lines 112, 231, 237, 261, 277, 355) so the described tests match what go test and the rg guard demand.
message: The contract's enumerated test changes contradict what its validation commands require. The narrative says affordances_test.go has 'all three' sites to update (wantNext builder, the assertNext line, human Next: substring), but the changed affordance (resolve.go:282 prompt_built / errors.go:87 / prompt.go:563) is the common source feeding six assertion sites: affordances_test.go lines 112, 231, 237, 261, 277, and 355 (status.Next at 261 and show.Next at 277 expose the same prompt_built next array; there are two assertNext lines, 231 and 355). Both `go test ./internal/...` and the rg guard require all six to change. An implementer who follows the 'all three' count literally will produce a failing gate (go test fails at 261/277/355; rg guard still matches them). The validation suite is sound and catches the gap, so this is advisory rather than blocking, but the contract's stated test scope is not self-consistent with its own validation and the undercount invites a wasted gate-fix round-trip. Fix: correct the enumeration to all six occurrences (lines 112, 231, 237, 261, 277, 355) so the described tests match what go test and the rg guard demand.

fingerprint: scope:the in-scope prose enumerates the affordances_test.go changes as 'all three: the wantnext builder, the assertnext line, and the human-output next: substring check', but the file actually has six assertions of 'pactum execute plan <run> --agent codex' (lines 112, 231, 237, plus 261 status.next, 277 show.next, and 355 a second assertnext in testnextaffordancesacrosslifecyclestages). lines 261/277/355 assert the same resolve.go affordance surfaced via 'status --json', 'task show --json', and a second lifecycle test, so dropping --agent codex from production breaks them too and they must be updated. the 'all three' count under-enumerates the in-scope test surfaces. this is advisory, not a blocker: the high-level scope.in file-level bullet, the 'no affected test still asserts --agent codex' acceptance criterion, and the rg validation over affordances_test.go all backstop the omission. recommend correcting the count to avoid steering a verbatim executor into a temporarily red build.
message: The in-scope prose enumerates the affordances_test.go changes as 'all three: the wantNext builder, the assertNext line, and the human-output Next: substring check', but the file actually has six assertions of 'pactum execute plan <run> --agent codex' (lines 112, 231, 237, plus 261 status.Next, 277 show.Next, and 355 a second assertNext in TestNextAffordancesAcrossLifecycleStages). Lines 261/277/355 assert the same resolve.go affordance surfaced via 'status --json', 'task show --json', and a second lifecycle test, so dropping --agent codex from production breaks them too and they must be updated. The 'all three' count under-enumerates the in-scope test surfaces. This is advisory, not a blocker: the high-level scope.in file-level bullet, the 'no affected test still asserts --agent codex' acceptance criterion, and the rg validation over affordances_test.go all backstop the omission. Recommend correcting the count to avoid steering a verbatim executor into a temporarily red build.

fingerprint: quality:the contract's load-bearing assumption is stated inaccurately and its real resolution fallback is left implicit. the goal, result paragraph, acceptance criteria, and assumption #2 all claim the bare `pactum execute plan <run>` 'respects pipeline.execute.by (the configured executor)'. but resolveexecutorentry is a two-tier fallback: it uses pipeline.execute.by[0] only when execute.by is configured, and otherwise resolves to config.agents[0] (the first registry entry). execute.by is optional/filterable and the empty case is explicitly tested (resolveexecutorentry with empty stageby -> first registry entry). two unsurfaced implications: (a) the 'claude-only user gets claude' promise is actually delivered by the config.agents[0] fallback for an unset-execute.by user, not by pipeline.execute.by; and (b) dropping --agent codex is not a strict no-op for every existing codex user — a user with execute.by unset and a registry whose first entry is not codex previously got a guaranteed codex and now gets config.agents[0]. the assumption should be restated as 'resolves to pipeline.execute.by when configured, otherwise the first registry entry (config.agents[0])', and the registry-order behavior change for unset-execute.by multi-agent registries should be acknowledged.
message: The contract's load-bearing assumption is stated inaccurately and its real resolution fallback is left implicit. The Goal, result paragraph, Acceptance Criteria, and Assumption #2 all claim the bare `pactum execute plan <run>` 'respects pipeline.execute.by (the configured executor)'. But resolveExecutorEntry is a two-tier fallback: it uses pipeline.execute.by[0] ONLY when execute.by is configured, and otherwise resolves to config.Agents[0] (the first registry entry). execute.by is optional/filterable and the empty case is explicitly tested (resolveExecutorEntry with empty stageBy -> first registry entry). Two unsurfaced implications: (a) the 'claude-only user gets claude' promise is actually delivered by the config.Agents[0] fallback for an unset-execute.by user, not by pipeline.execute.by; and (b) dropping --agent codex is not a strict no-op for every existing codex user — a user with execute.by unset and a registry whose first entry is not codex previously got a guaranteed codex and now gets config.Agents[0]. The assumption should be restated as 'resolves to pipeline.execute.by when configured, otherwise the first registry entry (config.Agents[0])', and the registry-order behavior change for unset-execute.by multi-agent registries should be acknowledged.

fingerprint: validation:the negative rg validation command treats rg execution errors the same as the expected no-match case, so the guard can pass vacuously if rg is unavailable or errors.
message: The negative rg validation command treats rg execution errors the same as the expected no-match case, so the guard can pass vacuously if rg is unavailable or errors.
